note: Risk scenarios derived from DSIT (2024), NCSC (2020-2023), and ICO (2023) data sources used in dissertation research

risks.description: Ransomware attack encrypting pastoral and safeguarding databases
risks.notes: DSIT (2024): ransomware incidents documented in schools. Schools have highest safeguarding obligations and weakest cyber resilience — the 'school vulnerability inversion' identified in dissertation Chapter 6.7. Primary school formal risk assessment rate: only 58% (DSIT 2025).

risks.description: Phishing attack compromising staff email — pupil welfare records exposed
risks.notes: DSIT (2024): phishing affects 92% of primary schools (among breached). Single compromised account may expose pupil welfare data. ICO (2023): 215 education-sector enforcement cases 2020-2023, many involving pastoral data exposure.

risks.description: Unauthorised access by former employee to student SEND and medical records
risks.notes: Credential management failure. SEND and medical records are UK GDPR special category data. ICO enforcement cases document similar failures. DfE (2024) KCSIE requires prompt removal of access for leavers.

risks.description: MIS system outage preventing attendance monitoring and safeguarding cross-checks
risks.notes: Dissertation Chapter 6.2: MIS failure prevents statutory safeguarding cross-checks. No manual protocol backup documented. KCSIE (2024) duties persist regardless of IT system status.

risks.description: Third-party cloud provider breach exposing FE college learner data
risks.notes: Jisc (2022): FE colleges increasingly dependent on cloud providers. Dissertation Chapter 2.2: supply chain attacks can propagate across multiple institutions simultaneously. Only 37% of FE colleges have dedicated cyber staff (Jisc, 2022).

risks.description: University research data breach — commercially sensitive IP and clinical trial data
risks.notes: DSIT (2024): 97% of universities breached. High threat severity but lower resilience deficit (92% have dedicated cyber staff per Jisc 2022) and minimal direct safeguarding impact. Illustrates SCRPM's frequency-vs-severity distinction from Chapter 4.5.

risks.description: Denial-of-service attack disrupting school website and parent communication
risks.notes: Reputational disruption but minimal direct safeguarding risk. Illustrates SCRPM's ability to correctly deprioritise high-visibility but low-safeguarding-impact incidents relative to hidden safeguarding risks.
